edge: Personalized federated learning (FL) involves balancing local model improvement and global model tuning, addressing the challenge of aligning personal and global objectives.
The approach in personalized federated learning is inspired by Bayesian hierarchical models, leading to the development of a self-aware personalized FL method.
Inspired by Bayesian hierarchical models, a self-aware personalized FL method is developed for balancing local and global models in federated learning.
Federated learning (FL) aims to train a global model that is distributed across clients rather than centralized in the cloud.
Federated learning transforms machine learning ecosystems by leveraging computation and data resources of billions of edge devices without raw data leaving the devices.
FL aims to train a global model that aggregates gradients or model updates from the participating edge devices.
Recent research in FL has significantly extended its original scope to address the emerging concern of personalization.
A broad term that often refers to an FL system that accommodates client-specific data distributions of interest.
Personalization often refers to an FL system that accommodates client-specific data distributions of interest.
Each client in a personalized FL system holds data that can be potentially non-identically and independently distributed.
Each client in a personalized FL system holds data that can be potentially non-identically and independently distributed.
Data held by each client in a personalized FL system can be potentially non-identically and independently distributed.
Each device hopes to improve its on-device model through personalized FL.
While the practical benefits of personalization have been widely acknowledged, its theoretical understanding remains unclear.
While the practical benefits of personalization have been widely acknowledged, its theoretical understanding remains unclear.
Existing works on personalized FL often derive algorithms based on a pre-specified optimization formulation.
Existing works on personalized FL often derive algorithms based on a pre-specified optimization formulation.
Explanations regarding the formulation or its tuning parameters rarely go beyond heuristic statements.
In this work, we take a different approach. Instead of specifying an optimization problem to solve, we start with a toy example and develop insights into the nature of personalization.
Personalization is examined to gain insights into its nature from a statistical uncertainty perspective.

The discussion mentions the need for the popular aggregation approach in the context of FL updates.
The popular aggregation approach mentioned does not account for the distribution discrepancy due to not sharing clients' data and estimating underlying data distributions being unrealistic.
It is highly nontrivial to measure and calibrate such a discrepancy in FL settings, which is a key motivation of the work.
The technical contribution includes interpreting personalization from a hierarchical model-based perspective and providing theoretical analyses for FL training.
Proposing Self-FL, an active personalized FL solution that guides local training and global aggregation via inter- and intra-client uncertainty quantification is another contribution.
Self-FL is the first work that connects personalized FL with hierarchical modeling and utilizes uncertainty quantification to drive personalization.
The term personalization in FL often refers to the development of client-speciﬁc model parameters for a given model architecture.
In this context, each client aims to obtain a local model that has desirable test performance on its local data distribution.
Personalized FL is critical for applications that involve statistical heterogeneity among clients.
A research trend is to adapt the global model for accommodating personalized local models.

Self-FL algorithm yields more stable convergence compared with FedAvg and the other three personalized FL methods due to our smoothing average in aggregation. Self-FL achieves the highest personalized accuracy when the global model converges.
